categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's historical returns, consistency, income contribution, risk-adjusted return quality, technical trend position, and how well it has tracked its benchmark or peers across multiple time horizons. Maximum-drawdown severity, capture ratios, and absolute downside-protection metrics are covered in Risk Analysis — this category stays focused on the return story itself. Factors are assigned to one or more ETF groups defined in etf-analysis-categories.json; each group uses exactly 5 factors from this file.

factors.factorDescription: Measures compound annual growth rate over 5, 10, 15, and 20-year periods. Shows whether the fund has consistently grown investor capital over extended horizons. For funds younger than 5 years, note the limited history and use only the periods available rather than extrapolating. For daily-leveraged or inverse funds, long-horizon CAGR is the clearest evidence of compounding decay — compare against the unleveraged underlying's CAGR times the stated leverage factor.

Evaluates recent performance over 1-month, 3-month, 6-month, YTD, and 1-year periods. Distinguishes short-term momentum (1M, 3M) from trailing performance (6M, 1Y). A strong 1Y return alongside weak recent months usually reflects a normal pullback within an uptrend rather than broad weakness.

Assesses how stable returns have been across different periods and calendar years. Flags wild year-to-year swings and tracks whether the fund recovered quickly from drawdown years. Uses percentile-rank trends across years to detect deteriorating or improving relative standing.
factors.factorMetrics: returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, percentileRanks

factors.factorDescription: Compares the fund's returns against its stated benchmark index across every available period. For passive funds, tracking within 0.5pp (equities, core bonds) or 1.0pp (high-yield, specialized) is expected — persistent drag beyond that signals structural cost or replication issues. For active funds, measures whether the manager's picks added value above the index net of fees. For sector and thematic ETFs, note whether the benchmark is a narrow sector index (most XL- series SPDRs) or a broader thematic index — tracking tightness expectation is the same.

Evaluates where the fund ranks among its Mor-category peers using percentile and quartile rank data across 1Y, 3Y, 5Y, and 10Y. Tracks whether the fund's standing is improving, stable, or deteriorating. Always report the peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is very different from median in a 10-fund category. When the peer group is mostly active managers and the fund is a passive index tracker, account for the structural tracking-cost headwind before concluding the fund is underperforming.

Separates income contribution from price return. For bond, muni, and income-focused alternative funds, most of the total return comes from distributions rather than price appreciation. Compares total return (return fields) against price-only change (change fields); the gap is the income contribution. A positive total return alongside a declining share price means income is offsetting price erosion — normal for the fund type, not a red flag. For muni funds, also frame the yield in tax-equivalent terms (state a bracket assumption) so retail readers can compare against taxable-bond peers.
factors.factorMetrics: return1y, change1y, return5y, change5y, return10y, change10y, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, divYears

factors.factorDescription: For daily-leveraged or inverse funds, checks whether the realized multi-period return actually matched the daily-leverage target (e.g. 3x, -2x) or whether path-dependency and compounding destroyed the multiple. The long-term return of a leveraged fund is almost never the leverage factor times the underlying's long-term return — the gap is the decay story that must be explained to readers. Flag leveraged/inverse products as short-term trading vehicles only.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, returnsAnnual, beta, beta1y

factors.factorDescription: For bond, muni, credit, and allocation funds, assesses how the fund performed across rising-rate years (e.g. 2022) vs falling-rate years (e.g. 2019, 2020). Long-duration government and muni funds suffer most in rising-rate years and gain most in falling-rate years. Credit-driven funds (high-yield, bank loan, preferred, EM debt) are partly insulated from rates via spread behavior but can still lose in risk-off rate shocks. Allocation funds inherit the rate sensitivity of their bond sleeve — compare the fund's 2022 loss to the mix of equity + bond index losses it would imply. Compare the fund's worst calendar year to its category and index: if the loss was in line with peers, the drawdown was asset-class-driven, not fund-specific.

Reads current price against moving averages (MA20, MA50, MA150, MA200) and RSI levels (daily, weekly, monthly) to judge whether the fund is in an uptrend, downtrend, or sideways range, and whether momentum looks stretched. Price above all four MAs with RSI rising signals broad strength; price below all four with RSI falling signals broad weakness. Monthly RSI above 70 is overbought; below 30 is oversold. Also uses distance from the 52-week high and 52-week low (and ATH/ATL when available) to place the current price in a longer range. For sector and thematic ETFs, trend position matters because sector cycles are the main driver of future return paths. For leveraged and inverse funds, technical position is decision-useful because their core suitability case is short-term directional trading, not buy-and-hold — use this factor to judge whether the current entry looks reasonable rather than to predict future returns.
factors.factorMetrics: stockPrice, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent

factors.factorDescription: Asks whether the returns the fund delivered were worth the volatility it took. Uses Sharpe ratio (excess return per unit of total volatility) and Sortino ratio (excess return per unit of downside volatility) to judge return efficiency, with beta alongside to show how much of the return pattern is market-driven vs independent. For allocation and target-date funds, risk-adjusted return is the core value proposition — a moderate allocation fund that matches 60/40 returns with meaningfully lower volatility is doing its job even if the absolute return lags pure equity. For alternative strategies (long-short, market-neutral, derivative income, managed futures), Sharpe and Sortino are the honest scorecard: a low absolute return with a materially better Sharpe than equities can still be a sensible diversifier. Compare to typical category levels — a Sharpe near 1.0 is strong for allocation and bond-like alternatives; below 0.3 for a multi-year period is weak regardless of headline returns.
factors.factorMetrics: sharpe, sortino, beta, beta1y, return3y, return5y, return10y
